title: Can quantum-mechanical description of physical reality be considered complete?
text:
Abstract
In a complete theory there is an element corresponding to each element of reality. A sufficient condition for the reality of a physical quantity is the possibility of predicting it with certainty, without disturbing the system. In quantum mechanics in the case of two physical quantities described by non-commuting operators, the knowledge of one precludes the knowledge of the other. Then either (1) the description of reality given by the wave function in quantum mechanics is not complete or (2) these two quantities cannot have simultaneous reality. Consideration of the problem of making predictions concerning a system on the basis of measurements made on another system that had previously interacted with it leads to the result that if (1) is false then (2) is also false. One is thus led to conclude that the description of reality as given by a wave function is not complete.

title: Can quantum-mechanical description of physical reality be considered complete?

title: Research Areas
text: My research lies somewhere at the intersection of optimization, geometry, and statistics. I am particularly interested in both the development and analysis of algorithms for different optimization and statistical tasks defined on non-Euclidean spaces, such as Riemannian manifolds and Wasserstein space. My work is motivated by applications in data science, machine learning, and large-scale optimization.

title: Ian McPherson
text:
E-mail
  
  
    
     LinkedIn
  
  
      ORCID
  

  
  


Welcome to my website! My name is Ian, and I am currently pursuing a PhD in Applied Mathematics and Statistics under the joint supervision of Mauro Maggioni and Mateo Díaz at Johns Hopkins University.



I am interested in utilizing classical mathematical structures to give provable guarantees in non-classical spaces. At the core, I am inspired by the interplay of optimization, geometry, and statistics through the frameworks of Manifold Learning, Riemannian Optimization, Optimal Transport, and Variational Analysis.
My current research focuses on developing theoretical and algorithmic foundations for manifold learning in Wasserstein space, nonsmooth Riemannian optimization, and scalable algorithms for low-rank semidefinite programming. I have also been fortunate to explore ideas in mean-field langevin dynamics to provide end-to-end guarantees for neural approaches to portfolio optimization.




(In-Preparation) Wasserstein Barycenters on Unknown Submanifolds of Wasserstein Space, with Mauro Maggioni   Given only a local sampler of a non-convex submanifold of Wasserstein space, is there an algorithm to compute the Fréchet mean on the submanifold for a fixed number of points up to arbitrary accuracy? 
(In-Preparation) Nonsmooth Riemannian Optimization with Inexact Information, with Mateo Díaz and Benjamin Grimmer   Can nonsmooth convex Riemannian optimization admit nonasymptotic convergence rates using only subgradients, first-order retractions, and vector transports? 
(Submitted 12/31) Neural Dynamic Portfolio Control with Provable Learning Guarantees, with Yizhe Huang, Rui Gao, Shuang Li, Luhao Zhang   Are there neural approaches to portfolio control that incorporate historical returns with end-to-end (algorithmic, generalization, and regularization) global guarantees?

title: Teaching
text:
Teaching Assistantships

Johns Hopkins University

Mathematics of Data Science (EN.553.747)  Fall 2025 | For Professor Mateo Díaz | Ph.D level course on high-dimensional statistics, optimization, and geometry.
Elements of Statistical Learning (EN.553.634)  Fall 2024 | For Subas Acharya | Undergraduate/graduate level course on the theory behind statistical learning.
High-Dimensional Approximation, Probability, and Statistical Learning (553.738)  Spring 2024 | For Professor Mauro Maggioni | Graduate-level course.
Bayesian Statistics (553.632)  Fall 2023 | For Professor Sergey Kushnarev | Undergraduate/graduate level course.
Numerical Analysis (553.681)  Spring 2023 | For Professor Mario Micheli | Undergraduate/graduate level course.
Dynamical Systems (553.691)  Fall 2022 | For Professor Yannis Kevrekedis | Undergraduate/graduate level course on nonlinear dynamical systems.



Tufts University

Linear Algebra (Math 70)  Summer 2022 | TA and Guest Lecturer for Curtis Heberle | Delivered five guest lectures.
Statistics (Math 166)  Spring 2022 | For Professor Bruce Boghosian | Undergraduate introduction to mathematical statistics.
Numerical Linear Algebra (Math 126)  Fall 2021 | For Professor Abiy Tassisa | Undergraduate introduction.
Introductory Statistics (Math 21)  Summer 2021 | TA and Guest Lecturer for Linda Garant | Delivered four guest lectures.

title: Teaching
text:
Instructor of Record

Geometric Toolbox for Data Science (EN.500.111)  Johns Hopkins University | Fall 2024. Designed and taught a survey course in Geometric Methods for High Dimensional Statistics.
Master’s Program Probability Review  Johns Hopkins University | Summer 2023, Summer 2024. Designed and led a review series on probability fundamentals for incoming Master’s students.
